section_text_parts: In the same way that you can declare more validations and metadata for query parameters with Query , you can declare the same type of validations and metadata for path parameters with Path .
roots.section_text: In the same way that you can declare more validations and metadata for query parameters with Query , you can declare the same type of validations and metadata for path parameters with Path .

children.heading_text: Order the parameters as you need
section_text_parts: Let's say that you want to declare the query parameter q as a required str .
And you don't need to declare anything else for that parameter, so you don't really need to use Query .
But you still need to use Path for the item_id path parameter. And you don't want to use Annotated for some reason.
Python will complain if you put a value with a "default" before a value that doesn't have a "default".
But you can re-order them, and have the value without a default (the query parameter q ) first.
It doesn't matter for FastAPI . It will detect the parameters by their names, types and default declarations ( Query , Path , etc), it doesn't care about the order.
So, you can declare your function as:
But keep in mind that if you use Annotated , you won't have this problem, it won't matter as you're not using the function parameter default values for Query() or Path() .
children.section_text:
Let's say that you want to declare the query parameter q as a required str .

And you don't need to declare anything else for that parameter, so you don't really need to use Query .

But you still need to use Path for the item_id path parameter. And you don't want to use Annotated for some reason.

Python will complain if you put a value with a "default" before a value that doesn't have a "default".

But you can re-order them, and have the value without a default (the query parameter q ) first.

It doesn't matter for FastAPI . It will detect the parameters by their names, types and default declarations ( Query , Path , etc), it doesn't care about the order.

So, you can declare your function as:

But keep in mind that if you use Annotated , you won't have this problem, it won't matter as you're not using the function parameter default values for Query() or Path() .

children.heading_text: Order the parameters as you need, tricks
section_text_parts: Here's a small trick that can be handy, but you won't need it often.
If you want to:
- declare the q query parameter without a Query nor any default value
- declare the path parameter item_id using Path
- have them in a different order
- not use Annotated
...Python has a little special syntax for that.
Pass * , as the first parameter of the function.

Python won't do anything with that * , but it will know that all the following parameters should be called as keyword arguments (key-value pairs), also known as kwargs . Even if they don't have a default value.
children.section_text:
Here's a small trick that can be handy, but you won't need it often.

If you want to:

- declare the q query parameter without a Query nor any default value

- declare the path parameter item_id using Path

- have them in a different order

- not use Annotated

...Python has a little special syntax for that.

Pass * , as the first parameter of the function.

Python won't do anything with that * , but it will know that all the following parameters should be called as keyword arguments (key-value pairs), also known as kwargs . Even if they don't have a default value.

section_text_parts: Keep in mind that if you use Annotated , as you are not using function parameter default values, you won't have this problem, and you probably won't need to use * .
children.section_text: Keep in mind that if you use Annotated , as you are not using function parameter default values, you won't have this problem, and you probably won't need to use * .

children.heading_text: Number validations: greater than or equal
section_text_parts: With Query and Path (and others you'll see later) you can declare number constraints.
Here, with ge=1 , item_id will need to be an integer number " g reater than or e qual" to 1 .
children.section_text:
With Query and Path (and others you'll see later) you can declare number constraints.

Here, with ge=1 , item_id will need to be an integer number " g reater than or e qual" to 1 .

children.heading_text: Number validations: greater than and less than or equal
section_text_parts: The same applies for:
- gt : g reater t han
- le : l ess than or e qual
children.section_text:
The same applies for:

- gt : g reater t han

- le : l ess than or e qual

children.heading_text: Number validations: floats, greater than and less than
section_text_parts: Number validations also work for float values.
Here's where it becomes important to be able to declare gt and not just ge . As with it you can require, for example, that a value must be greater than 0 , even if it is less than 1 .
So, 0.5 would be a valid value. But 0.0 or 0 would not.
And the same for lt .
children.section_text:
Number validations also work for float values.

Here's where it becomes important to be able to declare gt and not just ge . As with it you can require, for example, that a value must be greater than 0 , even if it is less than 1 .

So, 0.5 would be a valid value. But 0.0 or 0 would not.

And the same for lt .

section_text_parts: With Query , Path (and others you haven't seen yet) you can declare metadata and string validations in the same ways as with Query Parameters and String Validations .
And you can also declare numeric validations:
- gt : g reater t han
- ge : g reater than or e qual
- lt : l ess t han
- le : l ess than or e qual

flat_nodes.heading_text: Order the parameters as you need
flat_nodes.section_text:
Let's say that you want to declare the query parameter q as a required str .

And you don't need to declare anything else for that parameter, so you don't really need to use Query .

But you still need to use Path for the item_id path parameter. And you don't want to use Annotated for some reason.

Python will complain if you put a value with a "default" before a value that doesn't have a "default".

But you can re-order them, and have the value without a default (the query parameter q ) first.

It doesn't matter for FastAPI . It will detect the parameters by their names, types and default declarations ( Query , Path , etc), it doesn't care about the order.

So, you can declare your function as:

But keep in mind that if you use Annotated , you won't have this problem, it won't matter as you're not using the function parameter default values for Query() or Path() .

flat_nodes.heading_text: Order the parameters as you need, tricks
flat_nodes.section_text:
Here's a small trick that can be handy, but you won't need it often.

If you want to:

- declare the q query parameter without a Query nor any default value

- declare the path parameter item_id using Path

- have them in a different order

- not use Annotated

...Python has a little special syntax for that.

Pass * , as the first parameter of the function.

Python won't do anything with that * , but it will know that all the following parameters should be called as keyword arguments (key-value pairs), also known as kwargs . Even if they don't have a default value.
Keep in mind that if you use Annotated , as you are not using function parameter default values, you won't have this problem, and you probably won't need to use * .

flat_nodes.heading_text: Number validations: greater than or equal
flat_nodes.section_text:
With Query and Path (and others you'll see later) you can declare number constraints.

Here, with ge=1 , item_id will need to be an integer number " g reater than or e qual" to 1 .

flat_nodes.heading_text: Number validations: greater than and less than or equal
flat_nodes.section_text:
The same applies for:

- gt : g reater t han

- le : l ess than or e qual

flat_nodes.heading_text: Number validations: floats, greater than and less than
flat_nodes.section_text:
Number validations also work for float values.

Here's where it becomes important to be able to declare gt and not just ge . As with it you can require, for example, that a value must be greater than 0 , even if it is less than 1 .

So, 0.5 would be a valid value. But 0.0 or 0 would not.

And the same for lt .

With Query , Path (and others you haven't seen yet) you can declare metadata and string validations in the same ways as with Query Parameters and String Validations .

And you can also declare numeric validations:

- gt : g reater t han

- ge : g reater than or e qual

- lt : l ess t han

- le : l ess than or e qual

section_text_parts: FastAPI added support for Annotated (and started recommending it) in version 0.95.0.
If you have an older version, you would get errors when trying to use Annotated .
Make sure you Upgrade the FastAPI version to at least 0.95.1 before using Annotated .
admonition_nodes.section_text:
FastAPI added support for Annotated (and started recommending it) in version 0.95.0.

If you have an older version, you would get errors when trying to use Annotated .

Make sure you Upgrade the FastAPI version to at least 0.95.1 before using Annotated .

section_text_parts: A path parameter is always required as it has to be part of the path. Even if you declared it with None or set a default value, it would not affect anything, it would still be always required.
admonition_nodes.section_text: A path parameter is always required as it has to be part of the path. Even if you declared it with None or set a default value, it would not affect anything, it would still be always required.

section_text_parts: This is probably not as important or necessary if you use Annotated .
admonition_nodes.section_text: This is probably not as important or necessary if you use Annotated .

section_text_parts: Query , Path , and other classes you will see later are subclasses of a common Param class.
All of them share the same parameters for additional validation and metadata you have seen.
admonition_nodes.section_text:
Query , Path , and other classes you will see later are subclasses of a common Param class.

All of them share the same parameters for additional validation and metadata you have seen.

admonition_nodes.title: Technical Details
section_text_parts: When you import Query , Path and others from fastapi , they are actually functions.
That when called, return instances of classes of the same name.
So, you import Query , which is a function. And when you call it, it returns an instance of a class also named Query .
These functions are there (instead of just using the classes directly) so that your editor doesn't mark errors about their types.
That way you can use your normal editor and coding tools without having to add custom configurations to disregard those errors.
admonition_nodes.section_text:

These functions are there (instead of just using the classes directly) so that your editor doesn't mark errors about their types.

That way you can use your normal editor and coding tools without having to add custom configurations to disregard those errors.
